data.title: MNTN's IPO May Be Over - But The Opportunity Isn't
data.description:
With this analysis, I am beginning my coverage on MNTN, Inc. (NYSE:MNTN), a newly listed adtech company. In this analysis, I'll be providing an overview of the company, their business model, and recent financial performance. I'll also be offering color regarding their current valuation and highlighting the primary risks that I see for the company. In the end, I'll be recommending a 'Buy' rating for the stock for long-term investors, for reasons discussed throughout.

Company Overview

Advertising on television has always seemed like big business. And historically, it has been. Only the companies with the deepest pockets had the means to reach nationwide audiences via television commercials. The high costs and lack of precision targeting have created a rather narrow set of qualified advertisers.

Every February, headlines hit the news outlets calling out the absurd prices for 30-second ad spots during the Super Bowl. This past year, such a spot cost upwards of $7 million, or close to $600,000 per second of air time. The largest brands have ponied up for 30-second ad spots that cost more than the lifetime earnings of the average American citizen—several times over.

Headlines such as these have woven the narrative that advertising on television is accessible for only the largest of brands. Small and medium-sized businesses have been relegated to the position that they need to work themselves up the advertising ladder before they can take advantage of the unparalleled scale offered by television advertising. They allocate their advertising spend to social media, display advertising, email marketing, radio, print, billboards, etc. Then, only once they have reached critical mass, they can make the jump to advertising on television.

Traditionally, this has been the norm for the advertising industry. However, there are a select few companies that have begun shaking things up the status quo. One of those companies is MNTN.

MNTN was founded in 2009, coming out of the depths of the global financial crisis. The company is headquartered in Austin, Texas. MNTN's software enables businesses of all sizes to run advertising on television with the same ease that you'd expect from other forms of paid advertising, such as social media. Moreover, MNTN's software allows these businesses to run targeted, performance-driven campaigns—a task that has historically proved elusive in the big black box world of television advertising.

The company went public via an IPO just a few weeks ago. Shares had an offering price of $16, but the stock opened at $21. Shares continued to pop, as they jumped as much as 64% on the opening day.

Over the next few days, shares peaked close to $30. The market has since cooled down a bit on MNTN, with shares trading for ~$25. The company was looking to raise $187.2 from the offering.

Financials

Over the last four quarters, MNTN generated $246.3 million of revenue. This total is up 9.2% year-over-year. For context, in fiscal year 2021, MNTN posted just under $100 million of total revenue.

It's also worth noting that MNTN generates the core of their revenue from a take rate on total media spend on their platform. They also offer customers access to their in-house creative agency to help create ads, but this wouldn't be considered as their core revenue stream. While the take rate monetization model has its pros, it will be important to gauge the durability of MNTN's revenue should any macroeconomic headwinds arise.

From their revenue, MNTN posted a gross profit of 72%, indicating healthy unit economics. Moreover, MNTN produced a modest operating profit over the last year of $5 million. They are right in the middle of their transition to profitability.

Although they are yet to reach profitability, they are indeed cash flow positive. MNTN generated $52.6 million of cash from operations over the last year. In fiscal year 2024, they posted $42.5 million of cash from operations. In fiscal year 2023, this number was $18 million. Growth in cash generation from operations has been strong, largely due to operating leverage within the company.

As of their latest quarterly report, MNTN had $82.3 million of total cash and short-term investments booked on their balance sheet. However, I'd imagine this number is set to go up significantly following their IPO. They raised close to $200 million from the offering, so I'd expect their cash balance to be around $250 million come Q2 earnings. For an asset-light company generating around $250 million of annual revenue, this level of cash on their balance sheet will offer MNTN good optionality and serve as an initial cushion for riding the inherently volatile wave of the public markets.

Overall, MNTN's financials appear strong. Revenue growth has been strong leading up to their IPO. Continuing this trend will be key to sustained success in the public markets, too.

Valuation

With MNTN yet to reach profitability, revenue-based metrics provide the most context regarding their valuation. At the current price, MNTN is trading for an EV/Revenue multiple of 8.4x. I have to admit, this sounds a bit high considering their sub-10% revenue growth over the last year. However, it's worth pointing out that year-over-year revenue growth in fiscal year 2024 was much higher, at 28%.

An interesting company to compare with MNTN is The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD). Much like MNTN, TTD is a software-based ad buying platform that enables businesses to purchase advertising across various verticals including display, video, audio, television, etc. TTD is a much larger business in terms of magnitude ($2.57 billion of TTM revenue), but it can still offer color regarding MNTN's valuation.

Currently, TTD is trading at an EV/Revenue multiple of 13.7x. This is over 5 turns higher than MNTN's valuation. Moreover, fiscal year 2024 growth at TTD (25.6%) is similar to MNTN (28%).

Although I do believe that TTD's greater size and maturity should merit it a premium valuation relative to MNTN, I do believe that the delta between the valuations of the companies indicates that MNTN's valuation has room to run—assuming they continue to perform well.

To add to all this, I believe that MNTN could be an opportunistic acquisition target for a handful of companies. Possible acquirers that come to mind are TTD, AppLovin (APP), and the large tech advertisers including Alphabet (GOOG), Meta (META), and Amazon (AMZN). While an acquisition of MNTN's size would be difficult to move the needle for the largest three companies (Alphabet, Meta, Amazon), it could appear attractive for TTD and AppLovin.

In all, I believe that MNTN is trading at a discount. They have put together strong growth and appear to be well-received by the public markets. In a time when the IPO waters are far from smooth sailing, shares of MNTN have been a vote of confidence for the company.

Risks

As I see it, the primary risk for MNTN has to do with their line of business and their set of customers. MNTN operates in the advertising industry, and it's no secret that the industry can be inherently volatile. In times of economic distress, advertising spend is often one of the first line items scrutinized by companies. As such, MNTN's business is inherently cyclical.

Moreover, MNTN caters primarily to SMBs and mid-market companies. This segment of customers comes with its own perks, but they're also the ones that are often most susceptible to macro headwinds. When the seas get rough, it's often the smallest ships that have the hardest time navigating the rough waters.

Investors should monitor potential macro headwinds to get a better feel for the trajectory of MNTN's business. Additionally, investors should pay close attention to management's sentiment regarding these factors in their first earnings call as a public company in Q2.

Conclusion

In an industry that has long been reserved for the largest of corporations, MNTN has set out to democratize access to television advertising to even the smallest of businesses. They've been at this mission for over a decade and a half and have built a strong business. Top-line growth has been strong, and the company is on the cusp of profitability. In addition, MNTN's valuation appears reasonable relative to peers in the industry. As such, I am rating the stock a 'Buy' for long-term investors.

data.title: Koppers Meeting Its Aggressive Guidance So Far, But I Wait For A Lower Price
data.description:
Koppers' (KOP) 1Q25 results from last month were surprisingly in line with the very aggressive guidance issued at the end of 2024.

The company showed margin recovery in RUPS and CM&C, which compensated for a steep drop in PC segment earnings. This was all previously guided, and while results are volatile, they are not surprising. The quarter registered an accrual net loss, but this was largely driven by non-cash impairments and pension settlements.

With a more solid path to guidance achievement, Koppers may look undervalued on a forward P/E ratio. Still, I believe the long-term normalized earnings are lower than current projections. I maintain my view that the stock becomes attractive below $25 and remains a Hold for now.

1Q25 results

As commented across my coverage of KOP (see, for example, February 2024), the company's three segments average out margin cycles that are classical in industrial and chemical markets.

For FY25, management had guided in 4Q24 that Performance Chemicals (its most profitable and stable segment) would see a large decline in revenue and earnings due to increased competition, and that this would be offset by cyclical recovery in RUPS and CM&C. Back in 4Q24, I considered this an aggressive assumption. But so far in Q1, that playbook has held.

Starting with PC, the segment posted a sharp revenue decline and significant margin compression. This came primarily from the entry of new domestic competitors undercutting on price. Koppers is choosing not to match lower prices, and is losing share. This reflects typical cyclical dynamics (overcapacity leads to lower margins), but is especially concerning here because PC has long been Koppers' most profitable and consistent business. The hit to earnings is likely to last a few years. Additionally, economic uncertainty in the U.S. following "Liberation Day" tariffs led to a pause in residential construction and reform activity, adding cyclical pressure to a structurally weaker market.

Railroads and Utility Poles (RUPS), on the other hand, is delivering as promised. The company raised prices for railroad crossties and expanded utility pole volume, aided by the Brown Wood acquisition last year. These products are tied to long-term infrastructure needs, and demand tends to average out over time. After a weak 2023-24, demand is gradually recovering, and margins are improving from higher utilization and contract renegotiations. The mix shift toward utility poles, which have higher margins than rail, is also positive, as commented during the 1Q25 call.

The most positive surprise came from Chemicals and Carbon (CM&C). Despite continued top-line declines and global oversupply, EBITDA margins rose from 3% to 9%. This was driven by significant cost-cutting and the full exit from the phthalic anhydride business. While I continue to view CM&C as the structurally weakest segment, the improvement is notable and aligns with the company's strategy of simplifying operations.

Non-recurring factors: Two factors impacted the accrual EPS this quarter. One was the settlement of a pension plan, impacting $30 million in pension plan assets, at no cash cost according to the 10-Q. The second was a $20 million impairment charge, of which $13 million is non-cash (D&A acceleration) while the rest is cash-based (severance, consulting).

Questionable capital allocation priorities: Koppers' net debt increased by $60 million this quarter. Management attributed this to seasonal working capital build in Q1. That's consistent with industrial patterns, but it's worth highlighting that the company also repurchased $20 million in stock and paid $1.4 million in dividends. I do not support the decision to buy back stock while leverage remains elevated (3.6x EBITDA).

That said, CapEx is being tightly managed. The company expects only $65-70 million in net capital outlays for FY25, almost entirely for maintenance.

Still, I would prefer the company to use most available cash after maintenance to repay debt, and only when the leverage risk is reduced, to return cash to shareholders via dividends or buybacks. Leverage reduction is particularly important as the economy becomes more uncertain.

Valuation Is Reasonable, But I Remain Patient

Koppers is guiding to $4.75 in adjusted EPS for FY25. This includes adjustments for non-recurring items like the ones mentioned above, but not too much more. It is therefore approximately reflective of the company's expected operations this year. If this guidance is met, then at $32 per share, the stock is cheap on a 6/7x multiple.

However, I continue to believe that current margins are above the long-term average, particularly given that the most profitable segment is entering a down cycle. Based on previous periods (see detailed explanation in 3Q24 article), I estimate normalized earnings per share closer to $3.5. This is based on an average return on assets, adjusted for the company's higher asset base. However, this calculation is somewhat optimistic because it is based on periods when the PC segment was less oversupplied.

If margins revert further in FY25 or FY26, the current debt load will also become a bigger concern. Leverage is already elevated, and a drop in EBITDA could make refinancing or capital allocation decisions harder. I would prefer to see proactive deleveraging while cash flows remain relatively healthy.

Overall, I like where Koppers is going strategically and believe the market is starting to reflect a more realistic valuation. But I continue to believe that cyclical weakness will pressure the name further. It is very normal in cyclical industrial or chemical names for prices to really undershoot on the way down. For that reason, I maintain my Hold rating and prefer to wait for a drop below $25 before considering a position.

data.title: Verint Systems Q1 Earnings: The AI Narrative Gains Traction (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Verint Systems (VRNT) just posted a pretty average set of fiscal Q1 2026 earnings—but the stock jumped nearly 20% after hours. Why?

Two reasons.

First, the stock had been beaten down going into the report, trading at a 10-year low.

Second, there’s a new narrative: Verint is positioning itself as a player in the AI space, with over 50 AI bots aimed at automating customer experience workflows. These bots help clients save time and boost productivity.

So, you’ve got a setup where expectations were already low, the weak balance sheet and sluggish growth were priced in, and now there’s a fresh AI narrative.

All in all, there's enough here for me to upgrade the stock to a buy, from my previous neutral position.

Rapid Recap

Last quarter, back in March, I said,

[...] even though the stock is thoroughly cheap, at 7x this year's non-GAAP EPS, there are too many blemishes to this stock to allow me to keep my bullish rating on this name.

Looking back at that time, I didn't make the right call to have turned neutral on VRNT, as today VRNT is priced at more than $21 per share.

Verint Systems' Near-Term Prospects

Verint helps big companies make their customer service operations run smoother using AI. They’ve built a platform filled with over 50 bots that automate manual tasks customer service teams usually have to do by hand, and create significant value, quickly.

Verint makes the case that these bots don't require that customers rip out their old systems either — these AI bots are just plug and play systems.

So, businesses can start small and see real results quickly, thereby increasing their productivity as they scale.

Moving on, Verint declares that their AI business is picking up speed, with annual recurring revenue from AI growing 24%.

This strong growth was driven by two major deals, one with a big insurance company and one with a major healthcare provider.

Given this context, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Set to Improve

Take a look at the chart above, and you’ll see it plain and simple: Verint’s revenue growth has been all over the place. This isn’t a secular growth story—it’s a slow-moving business with lumpy results. And unless that changes, it’s going to keep trading at a low multiple.

That said, management is guiding for improved revenue growth over the next few quarters. If that plays out, the -6% y/y decline we just saw might be a near-term bottom, with things gradually picking up throughout the rest of fiscal 2026.

In fact, it wouldn’t be surprising to see mid-single-digit revenue growth next quarter and possibly double-digit growth by fiscal Q4 2026, especially as comparisons get easier.

Given this context, let's now discuss its valuation.

VRNT Stock Valuation -- 8x Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I'm drawn to businesses with a strong balance sheet. However, Verint Systems carries about $235 million of net debt; hence, on that front, it scores poorly.

Furthermore, I typically seek out companies with 5% net cash to market cap ratio. Whereas Verint Systems carries about 19% of its market cap as net debt, which isn't the sort of balance sheet I typically back.

Where Verint Systems does score positively is that the business makes a strong amount of free cash flow.

For their part, management reiterated that the business is likely to deliver approximately $145 million of free cash flow, a 12% y/y increase from the $130 million of free cash flow it delivered in fiscal 2025.

As a result, the business is now being valued at approximately 8x forward free cash flow.

This is very compelling, no doubt. Even as I recognize that the business's revenue growth rates aren't exactly enticing. But at 8x free cash flow, clearly there's a lot to like here.

Risk Factors to Think About

As I noted already several times, but I'll now make this risk factor more concrete, Verint Systems carries a large amount of debt. Case in point, there's more than $400 million of debt that will need to be tackled at some point.

Yes, Verint is removing its debt from its balance sheet towards its recently issued revolver, thereby avoiding having to raise convertible debt. But just because the debt is off the balance sheet, doesn't magically make this debt go away.

On top of that, management continues to use excess free cash flow to repurchase its shares. And while I know that stating ''[...] the largest use of our free cash flow to be stock buybacks'' sounds good in theory, in practice, management would be a lot better to deploy capital towards paying down its debt and strengthening its balance sheet.

Another risk factor is that Verint Systems doesn't operate with a large moat around its operations. The customer experience automation space has tons of vendors claiming they’ve got the next big thing in AI.

Furthermore, a lot of that hype has led to disappointing results for customers who bought bots that looked good in demos but flopped in real use. This means that the competition is tough, and potential customers are cautious after some bad AI experiences.

Thus, Verint Systems has to vie for market share against peers that are more nimble and with more resources, and possibly, even a superior narrative.

The Bottom Line

Looking back, I was too cautious last quarter when I shifted to neutral on Verint. Sure, the balance sheet still has its issues, and growth hasn’t been consistent, but the stock was just too cheap.

At 8x forward free cash flow and with management guiding to $145 million in FCF this year, the setup now looks far more compelling.

Add to that a gradually improving top line, a credible AI story gaining traction, and low expectations already baked in, and I’m comfortable upgrading Verint to a buy.

I now believe that this valuation gives me a fair margin of safety, even if execution isn't perfect.

data.title: Sekisui House, Ltd. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Sekisui House, Ltd. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: MIXUE Group: Riding On The Snow King's Global Brand Ambition
data.description:
We are bullish on MIXUE Group (OTC:MXUBY) ('MX'), China's largest beverage company by the number of stores, as we believe the company is well-positioned for China's consumption downgrade trend amid a soft macro backdrop, given its value-for-money leadership in several key categories such as ice cream, milk tea, and coffee compared with peers such as Starbucks, Luckin, and Chagee, driven by MX's robust and vertically integrated supply chain.

MX is traded over-the-counter (OTC) in the US, and its OTC shares largely track those of the Hong Kong shares. However, given that Hong Kong has greater liquidity, we recommend that investors trade Hong Kong shares over OTC. Investors can trade HK-listed MX shares through most brokers, such as TD, Interactive Brokers, or Charles Schwab.

Our bullish view is driven by the following factors:

First, MX's robust and vertically integrated supply chain allows MX to achieve a significant cost advantage across procurement, distribution and operations, which allows the company to deliver greater value perception on its products to the consumers, particularly in lower tier cities and Gen Z, that ultimately result in both GMV and volume leadership vs. its peers in China and abroad. We believe MX's prior investments in vertical integration will continue to drive economies of scale, allowing it to enhance product quality further while maintaining its price leadership.

Second, MX is a one-stop shop for purchasing value-for-money treats in key categories, including ice cream, coffee, and milk tea. This market positioning of providing a broad selection (i.e., three key categories vs. peers that focus on one or two), and clear price leadership in each category, can drive global scalability in both China and abroad. We expect MX to leverage its competitive moat to drive market share gains further, utilizing its franchisee-friendly policies and modular store design to consolidate the category markets.

Finally, we are bullish on MX's globalization potential, as it makes premium products accessible to the masses. When examining the ex-China markets, such as the US and the EU, coffee, milk tea, and ice cream are dominated by incumbent brands that focus on one or two key categories. On the ice-cream side, there are Dairy Queen and Baskin-Robbins. The coffee segment is dominated by Starbucks and QSR brands, whereas the milk tea segment is highly fragmented, mainly comprising small, independent shops. We believe MX can capitalize on the value-for-money demand across three categories in the overseas market, similar to how PDD's Temu has captured a largely overlooked discount e-commerce space.

Given that MX shares a very low correlation against the Hang Seng Index and China ETF, such as FXI, we also see MX to be a hedge against any volatility related to the uncertain Sino-US tension. We calculate that MX's correlation against the Hang Seng Index since its listing is 0.07, while its correlation with FXI is around 0.03.

We value MX on par with that of QSR names with product leadership, strong brand equity, and global scalability. Our target price of HK$757/share implies 30% upside from the current level, based on a 30x forward EBITDA estimate. (see valuation below).

Supply Chain Driving Low-cost Leadership

MX has one of the most vertically integrated supply chains within China's beverage sector, covering aspects from procurement to production and distribution.

On the procurement side, MX has a global procurement network covering 38 countries across six continents. The scale of the ordering allows MX to achieve significant leverage over its suppliers. In 2003 alone, MX acquired approximately 115,000 tons of lemons, 51,000 tons of milk powder, 46,000 tons of oranges, 16,000 tons of tea, and 16,000 tons of green coffee beans, which allowed the company to secure many core raw materials at prices below the industry average.

On the production side, MX was one of the first in China to establish centralized factories by venturing into the upstream to ensure both quality and consistency in the ingredients. Over the years, MX has built five production bases covering 0.79 million square meters to focus on the production of syrup, milk, tea, coffee, fruit, grains, and other condiments. As of Q3 of last year, total annual production capacity reached ~1.65mn tons, and the company possesses 124 patents that allow it to be one of the few brands in China in which franchisees procure 100% of their ingredients, packaging, and store equipment. This allows MX to achieve higher product consistency across its locations, where consumers can enjoy the same taste in the products across its key verticals.

On the logistics side, MX also has a nationwide self-operated warehouse system with 27 warehouses and logistics spanning 31 provinces, over 300 cities, and 4,900 towns across China. This is particularly important given that MX's presence is mainly in the lower-tier cities, and having an efficient logistics fleet ensures the precise flow of goods and services to the franchisees that meet the market demand.

As of Q3 of last year, MX has been able to reach 90% of the counties in mainland China within 12 hours, and the cold chain logistics currently covers 97% of the store networks in China.

Having a vertically integrated supply chain is accretive to the cost structure and allows MX to deliver higher value for money than its peers. We note that MX's procurement costs for milk powder and lemons of the same type and quality were approximately 10% and over 20% lower than the peer average, while the company also achieves roughly 50% cost savings on packaging bottles for syrups through its self-production method. The cost advantage is considered a competitive moat for MX and is difficult for other companies to replicate, which explains why many cannot move down the pricing ladder to compete against MX in terms of value for money.

Diversified Portfolio Driving Category Leadership

MX's low-cost advantage due to its supply chain efficiency has allowed the company to deliver value-for-money products across key categories in ice cream, milk tea and coffee.

This positions MX favorably amid the current macro environment in China and in the more established markets, where consumers are seeking comparable products at a lower cost. China's downgrade of the environmental environment has resulted in the consumer wallet shift towards value products, which partially explains the decline in sales by multinational brands in the country. On the other hand, the lack of low-cost alternatives outside China is creating an untapped opportunity for MX to capitalize on at a larger scale.

According to research firm CIC, MX ranks second among freshly made drink companies in 2023 globally in terms of the number of stores and cups sold behind company A, which we believe to be Starbucks, and ahead of company B, which we believe to be Inspire Brands, owner of Dunkin' Donuts and Baskin-Robbins.

In terms of GMV, MX ranks fourth globally, behind Starbucks, Inspire, and Company C, which we believe to be Restaurant Brands International, the parent company of Tim Hortons and Burger King.

MX's operating metrics are showing forward momentum across key indicators in GMV, volume, orders, and unit economics. Notably, GMV per order and per store has been stable over the years, which is impressive given China's cutthroat competitive environment and underscores MX's competitive standing relative to the other Chinese brands that have seen a decline in store margins and unit economics such as Chagee (see: Chagee: Brewing Big Dreams, Serving Cold Reality).

The rising volume per store, measured in orders and cups sold per day, also illustrates the growing demand for MX amid the current macro environment, and we believe MX could further accelerate these metrics as the demand increases over time or MX share from the mid-end beverage brands.

According to CIC research, China's freshly made drink consumption volume is still low relative to the other parts of the world in both volume and cups per capita.

As such, China is expected to be one of the fastest-growing regions in freshly made drinks behind Southeast Asia in the foreseeable future.

MX's Path to Becoming a Global Brand

We believe MX could become a global player in the value-for-money beverage vertical, much like Temu has become a category leader in discount e-commerce. The path towards MX's global leadership once again goes back to its ability to replicate its supply chain and scale it on a global basis, similar to that of Starbucks.

The early global expansion has seen some positive momentum, with MX operating 4,800 stores across 11 overseas countries. Approximately 70% of international revenue is concentrated in Indonesia and Vietnam. The proximity within ASEAN allows MX to leverage localized procurement to drive local innovation in beverages that cater to the local consumer base. In addition, the affordable pricing on beverages, and most notably on ice cream, has become a hit among the younger cohort, as we have seen in countries such as Indonesia and developed markets in Singapore.

We believe MX can be successful in ASEAN and gradually expand to more developed markets such as the US and EU, where MX's business model is subject to lower regulatory risk than companies operating within the high-tech space. We would keep an eye on MX's global expansion pace, particularly its store opening trend and overseas margins. We like what MX has currently accomplished and expect this momentum to continue in ASEAN in the foreseeable future.

Valuation Implies 30% Upside From the Current Level

MX is currently trading at 22x forward EBITDA, which on the surface seems to be a large premium against its global peers, such as Starbucks, that trade at around 18x, and even the domestic peers that trade between 10- 20x multiples. We believe MX deserves a higher multiple given that it is expected to achieve at least 20% EBITDA growth CAGR in the foreseeable future compared with its multinational peers that are expected to deliver low double-digit growth over the same period, and its domestic peers that are expected to deliver similar EBITDA growth rate but partially driven by cost-cutting given the maturing topline growth due to domestic competition.

We value MX at 30x EBITDA multiple, similar to that of QSR names such as Chipotle and Shake Shack that command a combination of 1) product leadership, 2) solid brand equity, 3) global scalability, all of which MX also exhibits.

At 30x forward EBITDA, we value MX at HK$757/share, implying 30% upside from the current level.

A key risk to our thesis lies in China's hyper-competitive environment. As China's macro profile worsens, beverage brands and QSRs are likely to resort to aggressive price cutting to drive traffic and user demand. Regardless of whether the price cut is on a long-term or short-term basis, we see any form of increased competition to be a negative for MX. Over the years, we have seen companies introduce unique ways to lower their ASP to drive demand, such as introducing smaller cup sizes that are priced at a similar rate to the cost leader. We are more concerned about outsized price cuts as their implication could be a negative to MX given its premium valuation vs. peers.

The other risk lies in globalization. MX has built a robust supply chain in China that can support its rapid expansion, but beyond Asia and Southeast Asia, its ability remains unproven. We believe a successful launch into the higher margin markets such as the US or the EU where MX is unlikely to face much geopolitical risk will likely be a major positive, but we question the capex commitment on scaling its supply chain and whether the business in the developed market could achieve similar value for money edge when compared against local incumbents such as QSR chains and branded coffee chains.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chart Industries And Flowserve: Selling Makes Sense, But So Does Buying
data.description:
In November 2022, Chart Industries (GTLS) announced that it would acquire air and gas handling product manufacturer Howden for $4.4 billion. To say the market disliked the deal would be an understatement: GTLS stock fell 35.6% the day of the announcement. After seven trading sessions, Chart had lost $5.1 billion in market capitalization, substantially more than the deal cost.

In retrospect, the first-day response from investors does not seem like an overreaction. At Tuesday's close, Chart stock had gained less than 5% from where it finished the day the acquisition was announced.

On Wednesday, Chart announced a second big deal: an all-stock merger with Flowserve (FLS), with an enterprise value of $19 billion. Financially, the two deals could not be more different. Howden was an acquisition, this is essentially a merger of equals. Chart levered up huge in the Howden deal, adding both debt and preferred stock; the all-stock deal with Flowserve should actually improve its balance sheet.

The reaction from the market, however, was directionally the same: GTLS fell 9.5% in trading Wednesday, with FLS down 6.2%. The bull/bear debate in each scenario looks somewhat similar as well. In each case, on paper the respective tie-ups made a lot of sense, with the post-announcement plunges making those fundamental arguments seemingly stronger. Yet in each instance, investors wondered why Chart was willing to essentially blow up its existing bull case for a business that looked less attractive than what it already had.

Though I'm remaining long here after buying the post-Howden dip, it does not seem comforting that the skeptics were proven right the first time around.

Was Howden A Bad Deal?

Or were they? Certainly, the GTLS stock price would suggest as much. The day before the Howden purchase was announced, Chart stock closed above $239. In the two and half years since, its high is $220. It seems prima facie obvious that Chart would have been better obvious returning capital to shareholders and/or paying down debt rather than stretching to acquire Howden. Had GTLS simply kept pace with the S&P 500 from its pre-acquisition level, shares would be above $350. They closed Wednesday at just under $150.

That said, it seems incredibly unlikely that a standalone Chart would have kept pace with the S&P 500. The optimism toward Chart in 2022 was driven at least in part by a 'clean energy' thesis (including around hydrogen under the Biden Administration), and that thesis has weakened notably over the past two-plus years. A pure-play peer for Chart is impossible to find, but it's worth noting that another hydrogen play, Plug Power (PLUG) is down 93% over this period. Other companies similar to Chart too have struggled. In energy more broadly, ChampionX (CHX) is off 21%. Air Products and Chemicals (APD) has returned a little over 5%, with basically all of that dividends.

Meanwhile, in business terms, the Howden deal did work out as management forecast. The projected synergies were achieved (Chart in fact did a little better, per the Q3 call). Chart guided for over 500 basis points of Adjusted EBITDA margin expansion within the first three years; by my numbers, those margins increased more than 600 bps between 2021 and 2024. Adjusted operating income more than quadrupled in two years.

The problem for GTLS stock, in other words, has not been profits, but rather the profit multiple:

To be sure, some of that compression comes from the fact that Howden was a lower-growth, lower-margin business (and that its price only appears to have represented a high-single-digit multiple post-synergies). But it's likely that at least a portion comes from the external environment, which has been rather weak for energy more broadly and clean energy more specifically. On the whole, the Howden deal was not as value-destructive as the GTLS stock price would suggest.

Is Flowserve A Good Deal?

In an environment where investors had more trust in Chart management (and likely Flowserve management as well; the fact that FLS generated 11% cumulative total returns over the decade leading up to the merger probably isn't helping confidence in the deal, either), it's not hard to imagine both stocks being up on the news. In fact, that's where shares of both Chart and Flowserve started the session before a steady sell-off to close at the lows:

The initially positive response makes some sense, because on paper (and yes, that's a big qualifier) the deal makes a lot of sense.

The two companies' product portfolios are largely complementary; Flowserve in fact currently supplies Chart with valves and pumps. Flowserve's stronger balance sheet pretty much completes the deleveraging for Chart post-Howden; net debt is projected to be 2x Adjusted EBITDA at closing, with the figure coming down as $300 million in synergies are achieved. Simply capitalizing those synergies at 8x suggests 15% upside (pro forma market cap is just shy of $16 billion, with Chart shareholders owning 53.5% of the combined business).

And after Chart was already "molecule agnostic" in the words of its executives, there's even more diversification for the combined business:

Meanwhile, the fundamentals here look solid. With the pullback Wednesday, the combined company now is trading at ~9.5x EBITDA (including the aforementioned synergies). Price to free cash flow, assuming a low 20s tax rate and capex at 2%-plus of revenue (the blended average for the two companies), is in the high teens.

Given what looks like overall mid-single-digit end market growth, that valuation looks reasonably attractive, particularly because the nature of the tie-up seems to de-risk the downside somewhat. That's true in terms of leverage and in a focus on higher-margin aftermarket revenue (42% of the pro forma total).

Why This Deal And Why Now?

In those contexts, the sell-off during Wednesday's trading doesn't seem to make a ton of sense. But the skepticism from the market can be summed up by the very first question on the post-announcement call from Baird's Mike Halloran: "why now from a transaction perspective?"

That question is an issue for both companies. Chart is coming off a Q1 in which orders jumped 17% year-over-year; the very first end market management discussed on that call was LNG (liquefied natural gas). Chief executive officer Jillian Evanko noted that one-quarter of Chart's backlog was in that end market.

More broadly, the bull case from management was that Chart was set to prosper, thanks to the completion of the Howden integration and broader energy demand tailwinds. Here's how Evanko framed the case at a conference in March:

And what we've seen recently is a ramp-up in global energy, and that's really being driven in part by data centers and artificial intelligence, which we're able to serve with our existing manufacturing capabilities. The increase in global energy [demand] serves our portfolio very well.

But with Flowserve, Chart is now levering itself much more to legacy industries (oil and gas accounted for 37% of Flowserve bookings in 2024, per the 10-K). As the presentation slide above shows, LNG is just 9% of combined company revenue; hydrogen (which has had some success outside the U.S. despite struggles domestically) and nuclear to get de-emphasized.

For Flowserve, too, there's a "why now?" question. As multiple analysts on the post-announcement call noted, Flowserve is in the midst of a simplification program, known as 80/20. And that company's management, too, had been talking up potential growth in the core of the business: on the Q1 call, its CEO, Scott Rowe, forecast 200-plus basis points of margin expansion by 2027 from portfolio optimization.

The answer from Evanko and Rowe on Wednesday was that these efforts meant the timing of the deal was actually perfect: as Rowe put it, "We've got positive momentum with both organizations." But you can see why shareholders of each company — betting more on growth with GTLS and value with FLS — see the deal as an admission that their previous theses were invalid.

Chart was a play on fast-growing sources of energy; that exposure is limited. Flowserve's 2027 margin target alone suggested at least 20% EPS growth for a company trading at less than 16x the midpoint of this year's guidance for that metric.

The thesis for the combined company is not quite as clean — and, at the very least, it's different from the case for each company on its own. From here, there's enough to like, particularly given the double-digit reversal from early-morning trading. But it is understandable why shareholders of both Chart and Flowserve see it differently.

data.title: Astera Labs Fits Into Credo's Playbook
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I first initiated coverage on Astera Labs (ALAB) just over a month ago, the stock was trading at $70.75. As of the writing of this piece, the stock is trading at ~$95. From a Quant rating perspective, the company has moved from being ranked 18/68 to attaining the 5th rank as shown in Figure 1 below.

Given that this is a newly listed company, this ranking tells you that numbers are improving and becoming better by the day. I mean, the company just recently reported some great numbers in their 1Q 2025 earnings results. Given that it's recently listed (listed in March 2024), investors may be adopting a "wait-and-see" attitude, which is completely justified. However, given its business operations, industry and secular growth, I would like to use Credo Technology Group (CRDO) as a barometer on how ALAB may turn out and provide some color around it. At least, Credo has been in the game for quite a while (I mean, as a public stock, longer than Astera); therefore, it only makes sense to use it as a gauge.

Astera and Credo are competitors, but I wouldn't look at them as such. In fact, I would think of them as "enablers." This is because the market they operate in is so huge that none of the companies would be able to single-handedly capture the whole TAM. The global market for AI services and products is expected to reach $900 Bn according to a report by Bain & Company. Secondly, the two companies make almost similar but differentiated and unique products. Thirdly, as I mentioned in a previous article on Credo, the company is fitting slowly into the Super Micro Computer's (SMCI) profile when it was trading at ~$80 back in October 2024. I believe Astera is following the same path given its strong Quant rating, management guidance on growth and analyst revisions in the last 90 days. This is why I am reiterating my strong buy rating with a price target of $140 within 12–24 months.

Why CRDO is a good barometer as to where ALAB will play around

First and foremost, both companies rank at the top of the semiconductor group. While Astera ranks 5th (a fast improvement from 18, 29 days ago), Credo ranks 2nd (has been at the 1st-2nd rank for almost 6 months now). It is also worth noting that both companies have a not-so-different market cap. While Astera is at ~$15 Bn, Credo stands at ~$10.6 Bn, so for sure these are almost look-alike companies.

Secondly, their factor grades are almost mirroring each other in some way. Growth, momentum, and revisions are at their best in both tickers, while profitability and valuation grades have room for improvement, as shown in Figure 3 below. Then there are their ambitious growth profiles and management guidance for upcoming fiscal years. Credo just released its 4Q FY2025 earnings, and revenues had increased by over 179% y/y. Credo's management expects an 85% increase in revenues in FY2026 to $800. On the other side, coincidentally, Astera reported a sequential 179% y/y in its 1Q FY2025 report. It is important to mention that in 1Q FY2025, revenues exceeded management guidance, with revenues reaching $159.4 Mn, which is a 13% increase from the previous quarter. The company now estimates a revenue guidance of $170 Mn in 2Q FY2025. As the Aries and Taurus products continue with their sequential growth, I expect more revenue contributions from them, and this should ideally improve the growth profitability grade as shown in Figure 3.

The comparative angle in ALAB and CRDO

While ALAB and CRDO operate in the semiconductor space, their products are highly unique and differentiated. And this is where the edge in each of the companies comes in, despite their numerous similarities, which are non-exhaustive.

First, Astera is more specifically focused. Its product families are focused on internal chip-to-chip connectivity for AI computers. On the other side, Credo is focused on external connectivity for data centers through its AECs and SerDes chiplets. While this may look or sound the same, it's actually very different. You see, Astera is focused "inside" a server (CPU, GPU etc.) and on the other hand, Credo is focused on many different servers in a data center and how they communicate with each other.

To put this into context for the non-tech audience, assume a data center has 1000 computers (very powerful servers), and each server has an average of 10 GPUs, then Astera's chips will help 10 GPUs within each server communicate with each other. And since its tech needs to be in each computer, then its chips will be managing connections between the cumulative 10K GPUs in that data center alone, hence requiring 10K ALAB chips. On the other hand, Credo is more external, so it will help the 1000 computers communicate with each other in the data center through their Active Electrical Cables (AEC) and SerDes chiplets.

In simple terms, Credo's products are more visible. Astera chips are not (unless you are a tech engineer and work in a data center, maintaining AI architecture servers.)

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, both companies score poorly in their valuation grade. In fact, apart from FWD PEG ratio, all other metrics are in the red, as shown below in Figure 4.

And that's where the story gets better. Both companies have their FWD PEG implying an undervaluation. Astera is trading at an FWD PEG of 1.24x, while Credo is trading at an FWD PEG of 0.45x. These numbers are lower than the sector median of 1.72x, suggesting a 27% and 73% undervaluation.

Remember, these are companies in their growth phase, hence the reason they are trading at quite high multiples and have poor profitability grades. Despite that, investors are focusing more on the forward outlook and this can be shown by their analyst upward revisions in Figure 5.

An important detail worth mentioning Astera Labs is that it just recently entered the Quant strong buy territory in late May 2025. And given its profile, it's not a one-time event. It's there to stay. For a long time. I say this because Credo entered the strong buy territory almost a year ago (alternating buy and strong buy ratings a couple of times within the year). And at that time, it was trading at ~$27. Since then, the stock has moved 170% higher. With the same principles in mind and new data coming in, I believe Astera will follow a similar path. It's just a matter of time.

Key Risks

Surprisingly, both companies suffer from similar risks. And one of the key risks that could adversely affect our investment thesis is customer concentration risk. Both Astera and Credo have a huge customer concentration risk. Astera's customer E accounts for almost 31% of accounts receivable, while Credo's largest customer accounts for 61% of revenues, as per the latest earnings report.

The risk is that if a large hyperscaler backs down from buying more of ALAB products, there could be a gap in terms of revenue growth. For instance, Customer E accounted for 31% by the end of 4Q FY2024 and had stopped buying from ALAB by 1Q FY2025.

As a mitigating strategy, both firms are working towards diversifying beyond their current client portfolio. Credo is planning on adding 2 more hyperscalers by the end of FY2026

...as hyperscale customers continue to ramp more significant volumes and as we expect to begin to ramp two new hyperscale customers in the second half of fiscal year '26

Dan Flenning, CEO, Credo Technology Group.

On the other side, Astera is expanding into creating other product families (such as the X family series) that are not necessarily bound to Nvidia (NVDA), which is the largest customer. This move should have a larger part of their revenues contributed by the other families, as noted in the latest report.

...But if you look at the X-Series, the available time today is nearly zero [indiscernible] outside of NVIDIA. So it's a very rapidly growing market for us, and we do estimate that it will be the single largest sort of product line for us.

...the X family shipments are just going to start - have just started in this quarter and will start to ramp later on this year, with the full volume of them really coming in 2026. So we do expect in the '26 and '27 time frame for the X family to become a larger contribution to our revenues.

Jitendra Mohan, CEO, Astera Labs

Final Thoughts

As I mentioned in the beginning, Credo is a perfect indicator of how Astera will play out in the future. There are many similarities between the companies, with a few but very unique differences. At the end of the day, both companies will become huge beneficiaries of secular AI growth prospects as companies begin to create the structures and processes that lead to meaningful value from GenAI, as per a 2025 McKinsey report. The growth prospects for Astera and Credo are ambitious but not far-fetched. The two have strongly positioned themselves as important enablers for AI. Literally, the two have some of the largest hyperscalers as their customers (if not all).

I believe it's not a matter of either CRDO or ALAB but rather a matter of CRDO and ALAB. Either way, one of them will be a standout performer. It's just a matter of time.

I reiterate my strong buy.

data.title: Impact Of Tariffs On Chinese Banks Likely To Be Indirect, Uneven
data.description:
US tariffs and the ensuing trade tensions are likely to indirectly impact China's state-owned, global systemically important banks due to their limited overseas exposures.

As of the end of 2024, the overseas operating income, as a percentage of total operating income, at Industrial and Commercial Bank of China Ltd. (OTCPK:IDCBY) was 9.7%, the highest among major Chinese banks, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. This figure was also the highest in at least five years for the lender, increasing from 8.5% in the prior year and 7.6% in 2020, the data showed.

China Construction Bank Corp. (OTCPK:CICHY) recorded the lowest overseas operating income exposure at 2.8%, unchanged from 2023 and 2020, according to Market Intelligence, while that of Agricultural Bank of China Ltd. (OTCPK:ACGBY) was 4.1%, compared to 6.7% in 2020.

China is home to five global systemically important banks (G-SIBs), including Bank of China Ltd. (OTCPK:BACHF) and Bank of Communications Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:BCMXY).

"The impact of the latest US tariffs on Chinese banks will primarily be indirect, transmitted through the effect on export-related sectors," said Yingrui Wang, China economist at AXA Investment Managers, in a May 29 email interview.

"The impact of tariffs will be uneven across the banking sector. Large state-owned banks are better positioned to absorb the hit, while smaller regional banks and those heavily exposed to manufacturing and export hubs may come under more pressure," Wang said.

China's banking sector is facing profitability pressure as the country maintains an easing bias on monetary policy with a low interest rate environment. Starting in early April, China and the US announced a series of tariff moves on each other, which eventually pushed the world's two biggest economies to declare that they would tax imports by as much as 145%. The announcements caused volatility in equity markets. The share prices of Chinese G-SIBs also tumbled.

On May 12, however, the US and China agreed to de-escalate by delaying the proposed tariffs by 90 days while negotiators worked on a trade deal, allowing the G-SIB shares to recover.

The US has sought to narrow its trade deficit with China in recent years. In 2024, China logged a $295.18 billion surplus from trading goods with the US, from a peak of $417.27 billion in 2018, according to the US Bureau of Economic Analysis.

"As generalized tariff hikes reach their limits, we expect the Trump administration's focus to shift to more targeted sectoral tariffs and nontariff measures to contain China's access to advanced technology and address national security concerns," Nomura said in a May 23 note. "Our US economics team views the current 30% tariff as the baseline scenario," Nomura said.

Uneven Impact

Ivy Ng, Asia-Pacific chief investment officer for DWS, told Market Intelligence in an email that tariffs are expected to impact banks' loan demand, leading to weaker growth.

Tariffs may raise concerns about nonperforming loans, particularly manufacturing loans with high export exposure to the US, even though the nonperforming portion is relatively small compared to banks' loan books, Ng said.

Chinese banks with offshore operations may face heightened regulatory and reputational risks, said AXA's Wang, while domestically, lenders such as joint stock and city commercial banks that have greater exposure to export-oriented businesses — particularly small and medium-sized enterprises — may experience slower loan growth and a deterioration in asset quality.

Under the current circumstances, systemic risks to China's banking system appears limited, and its lenders are relatively better positioned than certain peers in the region as they have lower risks on foreign exchanges and capital flow, Wang said.

According to the Bank for International Settlements, systemic risks may be defined as disruptions to financial services caused by an impairment of all or parts of the financial system. These risks have the potential to cause serious negative consequences for the real economy.

Major Chinese banks onshore are fairly insulated as their books are local, Chinese yuan-denominated and state-supported, according to Steve Alain Lawrence, chief investment officer, Balfour Capital Group. However, offshore stress remains.

But "systemic risk isn't imminent," Lawrence told Market Intelligence via email on April 25.

Any true fallout is unlikely as Beijing won't allow its financial system to show weakness on the world stage, Lawrence said, expecting swift state support, whether through capital infusions, backstopping offshore units or forced restructurings if pressure escalates.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Semtech: Buy Rating Initiated On Transition Catalyst Potential And Valuation Upside
data.description:
Buy the Transition: Inflection Catalysts Outweigh Risks

We initiate Semtech Corporation (NASDAQ:SMTC) with a Buy rating and a $52 PT. Semtech Corporation designs and manufactures analog and mixed-signal semiconductors for enterprise, communications, industrial, and consumer end-markets through a portfolio of FiberEdge, LoRa, power, and protection products. Our buy case rests on three conviction points:

1) Our FY26E/FY27E revenues of $980mn (+7.8% y/y) and $1.03bn (+5.1% y/y) sit meaningfully below consensus as we expect market-discounted headwinds from a prolonged CopperEdge recovery and an imminent $250-300mn IoT divestiture. However, we believe the LoRa and FiberEdge franchises will take over and unlock higher margins and EPS expansion in the face of deflationary top-line growth.

2) Valuation does not reflect the inflection set-up. We apply a conservative 4.25x/4.50x EV/ multiple to FY26E/FY27E to value shares, which is supported by historical averages, strategic peer group premiums, and the opportunity for re-rating as transition risks are cleared.

The debate: Can Semtech deliver operating leverage and embedded value capture as legacy business declines? Our view: Yes, with a margin of safety. We build into our model delayed ramp and IoT divestiture disruption as real but limited risks. Bottom line: R/R skews materially positive at current levels, with 34% upside to our $52 PT, driven by misunderstood transition catalysts and discounted valuation.

LoRa and FiberEdge = Margin Upside Engines in Portfolio Transition

The arithmetic is compelling: Semtech's transition to LoRa and FiberEdge is structurally altering the margin profile of the company at a time when investors appear fixated on legacy and IoT transitions. In the latest quarter, Semtech delivered a non-GAAP adj GM of 53.5% (370 bps y/y expansion) while adj OM came in at 19.0% (up from 12.2% a year ago). Importantly, this was achieved with company-wide revenues essentially flat sequentially - a testament to the margin mix skewing toward LoRa and FiberEdge franchises.

The math is straightforward: LoRa-enabled solutions delivered healthy revenues trajectory at $38.9mn in industrial (+81% y/y) while FiberEdge (within data center solutions) contributed at record levels - anchoring revenues growth in 400G/800G optics where Semtech has a technological advantage. As these businesses scale up, their higher GMs and operating leverage disproportionately influence company-wide margins. Strategic R&D investments are being prioritized in these areas. It's a flywheel - the more the margin-accretive businesses get funded, the further they pull ahead of the lower-margin, legacy operations.

The upside case is compelling: as LoRa and FiberEdge grow as a portion of the portfolio, investors should see durable margin expansion that supports earnings growth - even as headline top-line growth normalizes post-divestiture. This margin trajectory should drive a valuation re-rating as the transition risk subsides. This is an attractive entry point for return-focused investors who can look past the near-term noise.

Valuation dislocation needs to resolve as transition noise dissipates

The opportunity is real: Semtech's equity valuation continues to price in outsized risk from lingering legacy portfolio headwinds, leaving shares at an attractive entry point vs. both its historical range and the peer group. As of the latest period end, Semtech trades at 3.7× EV/Sales on FY26E revenue, substantially below the 5-year average range of 4.4–5.2× pre-pivot from the low-growth IoT and CopperEdge segments. For context, even with management calling out transition-related volatility (i.e., IoT divestiture and lagging CopperEdge demand), the Board authorized a new $150mn share repurchase program in May, indicating confidence that the market is over-discounting the pace and durability of the margin reset.

What makes this attractive is that as legacy headwinds subside, both The street and market perception will shift from top-line softness to the margin-accretive impact of LoRa and FiberEdge — segments already benefiting from higher growth rates and capital allocation. Historically, comparable analog/mixed-signal semiconductor companies have experienced 20–30% multiple expansion in the 6–12 months following a portfolio transformation, especially when strategic divestitures are combined with visible operating leverage.

We see a clear catalyst for re-rating as visibility improves: with transition noise fading and capital returns resuming, the market will likely reward Semtech with a more normalized multiple, unlocking significant upside for investors willing to position ahead of the cycle.

Semtech: The street Too Bullish; Transition Risks Under-estimated

We est FY26E/FY27E revenues of $980mn (+7.8% y/y)/$1.03bn (+5.1% y/y), meaningfully below the street of $1.03bn/$1.13bn, as we believe the street has yet to under-estimate (i) a more muted return of CopperEdge, given an anchor-customer rack re-design, which pushes a meaningful comeback to at least late FY26E, and (ii) management's stated intent to divest IoT-sys by mid-FY26E, which effectively takes $250-300mn of revenues off the run-rate in one fell swoop.

Source: FMP FY26 FY27
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $1.03B 10 $1.13B 13
EBITDA $-55.43M 10 $-60.53M 13
Net Income $121.24M 10 $157.44M 13
EPS $1.66 9 $2.14 11
SG&A Expenses $270.66M 10 $295.58M 13

Meanwhile, core LoRa (+205% y/y in Q4) and FiberEdge franchises will be strong but not enough to fill the structural revenue gap. Our sense is that the street models carry the full-year IoT contribution through FY27E, and are 8-10% too high on forward revenues. While mix and op. leverage should provide a credible margin story, our conviction is that there can be upside to EPS with top-line misses, a recipe to surprise momentum investors.

Valuation

Our $52 PT for Semtech is based on a 4.50x EV/Sales multiple applied against our continued FY27E revenue estimate of $1.0bn, a framework which explicitly insulates both the transitional risk and underappreciated re-rating catalysts. Our multiple equates to a calculated, but still modest, premium to the current 3.8x level (below the 4.8x median and 23rd pctile of STC's historical range, but above 4.1x the peer median) which we continue to see as appropriate given: (1) the commercial ramp of LoRa and FiberEdge and (2) credible near-term margin expansion catalysts as the legacy/IoT businesses run off.

We believe the continued focus on headline revenue risk leaves the more sustainable structural reset 'in the weeds'—EPS and Op Leverage expansion ahead as the top-line moderates—which remains underappreciated in consensus long-term models. With FY27E growth of +5.1% below the street expectations and transition volatility still a real (if bounded) overhang, we see asymmetric R/R at today's discount. Our conviction is predicated on our view that current valuation levels not only fully reflect downside from CopperEdge and the IoT divestiture, but systematically mis-price the margin and capital allocation upside unlocked post-transition.

Risks to our Buy rating

In what we view as a compelling mix-driven margin expansion opportunity as Semtech transitions towards LoRa and FiberEdge, we see a partially derailed thesis from two main risk factors:

1. Our base case assumes timely completion of an IoT-systems divestiture in FY26E, removing $250-300mn of largely low-margin hardware revenue and enabling mix improvement--but a failed or delayed sale could keep the underperforming segment in the mix, maintaining a drag on margins and cash flow longer than modeled.

2. We model a gradual CopperEdge recovery, but an earlier-than-expected re-acceleration at key hyperscaler customers (through design-in wins and/or a macro data center CapEx ramp) could lead to The street revenue upside that reduces the size of the operating leverage inflection, and vice versa for a slowdown.

Both of these transition-related variables are compounded by intense competition in SMTC's target end markets - spanning data center, IoT, and industrial - where larger analog / mixed-signal peers have significant scale and pricing power, with further downside if export restrictions or macro shocks disrupt LoRa demand in China or supply chains more broadly.

We are watching CopperEdge channel inventories, public statements on the IoT asset sale process, and LoRa chip sell-through rates for early signals of deviation from our thesis. If Semtech faces prolonged IoT hardware drag or misses operational timing on portfolio reshaping, the anticipated EPS and valuation reset may take longer to unfold than our base case assumes.

Conclusion

We believe Semtech remains a Buy as we see markets overvalue transitional rev noise while underestimating LoRa and FiberEdge-driven margin expansion opportunity. Our thesis is based on the belief that operating leverage, portfolio simplification, and eventual re-rating will provide upside discounted by current consensus despite headline volatility near term. We believe risk/reward is significantly skewed to the upside from here - investors will need to look beyond transitory rev dislocation and focus on evidence of a successful IoT divestiture and margin expansion tailwind as the catalyst for outsized returns.

data.title: General Motors: Navigating The Trade War Storm, Reducing My Price Target
data.description:
The TACO Trade has gripped Wall Street. That's the "Trump Always Chickens Out" mantra that some investors have adopted. It's worked so far, with the S&P 500 notching fresh multi-month highs this week. The All-Country World Index (ACWI) actually tagged a new all-time high on Wednesday, too.

For companies most directly impacted by President Trump's trade policy. However, it has been a tough year. Shares of General Motors (NYSE:GM) are down 8%, including dividends, so far in 2025, underperforming the S&P 500 by about 10 percentage points.

I had a buy rating on GM last November, and the stock is down 17% since then, posting 16 percentage points of negative alpha to the US large-cap index. Still, I reiterate a buy rating today, while lowering my price target. I'll also provide a refresh on the technicals as we head into the summer months, which are sure to feature more headlines from the Tariff Man.

GM Underperforming the S&P 500 YTD

Back in April, GM reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $2.78 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $2.66, while revenue of $44.0 billion, up 2.3% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $810 million beat.

Shares merely wobbled after the release, though, as the focus was on the here and now amid volatile tariff headlines. Looking ahead to the Q2 report, the options market has priced in a 6% earnings-related stock price swing, with implied volatility dipping to 30% today.

Zooming in on the quarter that was, it wasn't all good figures. Its adjusted EBIT fell by 9.8% to $3.49 billion, while its margin narrowed to 7.9%. Adjusted free cash flow, while healthy at $811 million, dipped from $1.09 billion in the same quarter of 2024. Weakness was particularly evident in its North American earnings, though wholesale vehicle sales rose, and its market share ticked higher to 16.5%.

Preventing a bullish interpretation of the Q1 report was weak guidance. The management team revised its FY 2025 outlook lower, citing a $4-$5 billion cost impact from tariffs. GM imports a significant number of vehicles from Korea, Mexico, and Canada, along with importing parts from other countries for US production.

GM's Latest Guidance

The onus is on GM to mitigate those higher expenses through efficiency gains. Overall, though, the guidance set was not as bad as some analysts had feared-GM updated its FY EBIT to be in the range of $10-$10.2 billion as it scales up its full-size pickup truck production.

On the earnings outlook, the street has brought down its forecast for FY 2025 EPS. Last November, that tally was above $10, but it's now barely above $9. Hence, I am lowering my intrinsic value target. Bottom-line growth is then seen in the low single digits from the out year through 2027.

Not surprisingly, we've seen a bevy of sell side EPS downgrades, to the tune of 20 to 1 negative. Still, free cash flow per share is very high at $12.28 over the past 12 months, resulting in a FCF yield of a whopping 25%.

GM: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key adjustments. If we assume a 7.1x price-to-earnings multiple (its long-term average) and assume normalized EPS of $9.40, then shares should trade near $66. That's down from a $77 fair value estimate I calculated previously.

Shares also trade quite attractively on a price-to-sales basis amid severe policy risks.

GM: Remains Cheap on Earnings and Sales

Key risks for GM include growth that depends on strength in pickups and EVs-if we see weakness there, then profitability trends may deteriorate in the quarters ahead.

Moreover, tariffs and volatile trade policy make long-term investment decisions very difficult for GM's executives, and a protracted trade war will undoubtedly be harmful to profitability trends. GM must also keep watch on rising fixed costs and price increases elsewhere in the supply chain.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q2, 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, July 22 BMO. Before that, the company is slated to present at the Deutsche Bank Global Auto Industry Conference 2025 on June 11.

Shares also trade ex a $0.15 dividend on Friday, June 6.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

GM hit its $60 target that I called out previously, but it has been a tough slog in recent months. Notice in the chart below that shares have fallen below their long-term 200-day moving average. That trend indicator line is now flat in its slope after a quarters-long increase, indicating the bears are grabbing some control over the primary trend. Still, long-term support was defended nicely by the bulls at the depths of the post-Liberation Day selloff. Now, key resistance appears at a few levels.

First, $51 is the May rebound high. Next, $53 is the March peak. Finally, $55 is the YTD zenith from January. There's now a significant amount of volume by price above today's level, so the bulls have their work cut out for them. Making matters worse is the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart-it's merely in a neutral range; I'd like to see price rally through the mid-$50s on improved momentum to confirm a resumption of the uptrend off the November 2023 low.

For now, support is near $42 while $51 is resistance.

Shares Continue to Correct Off the 2024 Peak

The Bottom Line
